file_name: Aftab et al. - 2026 - A novel deep semantic- and vision-based self-attention architecture for skin cancer classification.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning architectures, specifically DeepLab V3 with a ResNet-18 backbone for skin lesion segmentation and a novel FusedNet (Inverted self-attention with Vision Transformer) for lesion classification, to obtain results on the HAM10000 dataset for skin cancer diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'Original research article' in the header. It presents a novel deep learning architecture (FusedNet/ISAwViT) for skin cancer classification, describes specific methods (DeepLab V3, ResNet-18, Bayesian Optimization), reports experimental results on the HAM10000 dataset (97.5% accuracy), and includes ablation studies and statistical analysis, which are characteristic of original experimental research rather than a review.

title: A novel deep semantic- and vision-based self-attention architecture for skin cancer classification
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel deep learning architecture, FusedNet (ISAwViT), for skin cancer classification and lesion segmentation using dermoscopic images. The system employs DeepLab V3 with a ResNet-18 backbone for segmentation, optimized via Bayesian Optimization, and a fused inverted self-attention Vision Transformer for classification. Experiments were conducted on the HAM10000 dataset, utilizing data augmentation and a 50-50 train-test split to address class imbalance. The model achieved an accuracy of 95.16% for lesion segmentation and 97.5% for lesion classification. Interpretability was ensured using LIME and Grad-CAM techniques to validate the model's decision-making process. The results indicate that the proposed model is more effective and efficient compared to recent techniques.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification; 6705 images total; identified as the class with the highest error rate in some classifiers.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset; 327 images total; identified as having the lowest error rate for correct predictions among some classifiers.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset; 115 images total; minority class with severe class imbalance; achieved 88% precision with a 12.3% miss rate.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset; 514 images total; categorized as benign in the introduction.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset; 115 images total.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset; 1099 images total; also referred to as seborrheic keratosis in standard terminology.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset; 1113 images total; categorized as malignant; achieved 96.2% sensitivity with 21 false negatives.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (e.g., melanoma, benign keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a FusedNet (ISAwViT) architecture for lesion type classification using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (7 classes) using Softmax and various neural network classifiers (WNN, NNN, MNN, BNN, TNN). Performance is measured by accuracy (97.5%), precision, sensitivity, and AUC.

The paper implements DeepLab V3 with a ResNet-18 backbone for semantic segmentation of skin lesions from dermoscopic images. The task involves distinguishing lesion regions from healthy skin regions. Performance is measured by segmentation accuracy (95.16%).

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine with 10,000 training images)
datasets_used.description: A large public dermoscopy image dataset available through the ISIC repository, consisting of 10,015 images to identify skin lesions with pigmentation. It is characterized by severe class imbalance and high intra-class/low inter-class variation.

use.training: Used for training the DeepLab V3 segmentation model and the FusedNet (ISAwViT) classification model. A 50-50 train-test split was applied, resulting in 5007 images for training. Data augmentation (contrast stretching, flips, rotations) was applied to the training set to handle class imbalance.
use.validation: 5-fold cross-validation was employed to validate model robustness and assess performance.
use.testing: Used for testing the final model performance, with 5007 images held out in the 50-50 split. The model achieved 95.16% accuracy for segmentation and 97.5% for classification on this set.
use.other: Used for interpretability analysis via LIME and Grad-CAM to visualize decision-making processes.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To validate model robustness and assess performance across different data partitions.
To compute 95% confidence intervals for accuracy measurements, using 1000 iterations.
To confirm statistical significance by comparing the best-performing classifier (WNN) with other classifiers based on 5-fold cross-validation results.
To indicate the consistency of performance across different data partitions and validate the reliability of accuracy measurements.
To measure the variability of performance metrics (precision, sensitivity, F1-score, accuracy) across different data partitions.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in performance between the best-performing classifier (WNN) and the other classifiers (NNN, MNN, BNN, TNN).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in performance between the best-performing classifier (WNN) and the other classifiers.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paired t-tests were used to confirm statistical significance by comparing WNN with other classifiers based on 5-fold cross-validation results. A p-value less than 0.05 indicated that the difference occurred by chance with less than 5% probability, defining significance. WNN was found to be statistically superior to NNN (p=0.008), MNN (p=0.041), BNN (p=0.002), and TNN (p=0.002).

segmentation.notes: Training accuracy improved with epochs: 87.56% (10 epochs), 88.24% (15 epochs), 93.97% (50 epochs). Best training accuracy was 93.97% with 6.03% error rate.

classification.metric: Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, F1-Score, AUC, Computation Time
classification.notes: WNN was statistically superior to NNN (p=0.008), MNN (p=0.041), BNN (p=0.002), and TNN (p=0.002). For melanoma (MEL), sensitivity was 96.2% with 21 false negatives. For dermatofibroma (DF), precision was 88% with a 12.3% miss rate.

architecture_used.main_model: FusedNet (ISAwViT) for classification; DeepLab V3 with ResNet-18 backbone for segmentation
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet-18 pre-trained models used for segmentation; Transfer Learning (TL) with CNNs mentioned for classification context

architecture_details.isar_design: Inverted residual blocks with embedded multi-head self-attention mechanisms; grouped convolutions; depth-wise convolutional layer

training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Step decay reducing by a factor of 0.1 every 15 epochs
training_details.optimization_method: Bayesian Optimization (BO) with Expected Improvement acquisition function and Gaussian Process surrogate
training_details.augmentation: BiHE contrast enhancement (100%); geometric transformations (horizontal/vertical flip, 90° rotation) with 75% probability
training_details.evaluation_metric_for_optimization: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) for segmentation hyperparameter tuning

classification_or_output_head.fusion_head: Flatten layer followed by fully connected layers

classifier_details.MNN: Two hidden layers with 25 and 10 neurons
classifier_details.NNN: Two hidden layers with 10 neurons each

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: 10.4 M total trainable parameters for FusedNet (ISAwViT)

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing/downsampling from 650 × 450 to 240 × 240 pixels for segmentation input
Geometric transformations: left flip, right flip, and 90-degree rotation applied to the training set

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Contrast stretching applied to 100% of training samples to enhance image contrast.
Horizontal/vertical flips and 90-degree rotations applied with 75% probability to the training set.
Used to handle significant class imbalances in the HAM10000 dataset; the training set was expanded from 5008 images to 49,881 images.

conclusions: The proposed FusedNet (ISAwViT) architecture for skin lesion classification achieved an accuracy of 97.5% on the HAM10000 dataset, outperforming other classifiers (NNN, MNN, BNN, TNN) and ablation variants (ViT-only, Inverted Self-Attention-only, features-level fusion).
The DeepLab V3 segmentation model with a ResNet-18 backbone achieved a segmentation accuracy of 95.16% on the HAM10000 dataset.
The Wide Neural Network (WNN) classifier was statistically superior to other neural network classifiers (NNN, MNN, BNN, TNN) with p-values of 0.008, 0.041, 0.002, and 0.002 respectively.
The model demonstrated high efficiency with a computation time of 54.8 ms per image (18.2 FPS) for the FusedNet + WNN configuration.
Interpretability analysis using LIME and Grad-CAM confirmed that the fused model correctly identifies relevant features for classification.
The model faced challenges with minority classes, specifically dermatofibroma (DF), which had a lower precision of 88% and a 12.3% miss rate due to class imbalance.
Melanoma (MEL) classification achieved a sensitivity of 96.2% with 21 false negatives.
The proposed model is considered more effective and efficient compared to recent techniques and pre-trained models like AlexNet (86.4%), VGG19 (85.9%), and InceptionV3 (92.5%).
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which exhibits significant demographic bias (54% male, 45% female) and underrepresentation of darker skin phototypes (Fitzpatrick IV–VI), limiting generalizability to diverse populations.

Severe class imbalance persists despite augmentation; the minority class dermatofibroma (115 images) achieved only 88% precision with a 12.3% miss rate, posing clinical risks for rare but critical diagnoses.

XAI analysis (LIME/Grad-CAM) revealed that in 33 of 127 misclassified cases, the model focused on spurious image artifacts (hair, gel, rulers) rather than pathological features, indicating potential learning of dataset-specific biases.

There is a noted mismatch between XAI visualizations and actual predictions: 78 misclassified instances had favorable XAI visualizations highlighting the lesion area, suggesting that explainability outputs do not guarantee diagnostic correctness.

The paper acknowledges a potential 5–12% performance degradation due to domain shift when applied to images from varying devices and lighting conditions, highlighting the need for prospective multi-institutional validation.
Annotation bias in HAM10000 (10–15% inter-rater variability in boundary delineation) may propagate to the model's decision boundaries, a limitation not fully mitigated by the proposed methods.
The model achieved 96.2% sensitivity for melanoma but with 21 false negatives, which is a critical limitation for a diagnostic tool where missing malignant cases is high-risk.

While the model is computationally efficient (18.2 FPS), the reliance on synthetic augmentation for minority classes may lead to overfitting to augmentation artifacts rather than genuine clinical patterns.
